mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers gunpowder weapons (A.2), specifically small arms including muskets, rifles, and pistols (A.2.d), their ammunition including smokeless powder propellants and bullets (A.2.e), and rifled barrel ballistics.

Directly addresses personal armor from prehistoric leather and mail to medieval plate and modern ballistic composites (B.1.a, B.1.b), as well as armor for vehicles covering warships and armoured fighting vehicles relevant to C.2 and C.1.d.

Directly and comprehensively covers outline B.2 on fortification, spanning field fortification (B.2.a), permanent pre-gunpowder masonry (B.2.b), early gunpowder-era low-profile designs (B.2.c), modern concrete bunkers (B.2.d), and the evolutionary drivers of each period.

Provides the broadest overview of the offensive weaponry section (A) across all sub-categories from hand-powered to nuclear and chemical-biological weapons, and addresses defensive counterparts in B; serves as a top-level entry for the entire section.

Covers fixed-wing aircraft and rotorcraft (C.4.a, C.4.b) including propulsion types (internal combustion, jet, gas turbine) and configurations directly corresponding to the aircraft weapon platform subsections of the outline.

Covers self-propelled missiles and rockets including rocket propulsion (A.4.b), guidance (free-flight and inertial, A.4.c), payloads and warheads (A.4.d, A.4.e), and the military origins of rocketry from 13th-century China to modern ballistic missiles.

Gunpowder (black powder) is the foundational propellant for gunpowder weapons (A.2) and its role as a low explosive versus high explosives is directly relevant to propellants (A.2.e.i) and the explosives category (A.3).

As one of humanity's oldest cutting tools and combat weapons, the knife belongs to early hand-powered shock/edged weapons (A.1.b), tracing construction materials from stone through metal across the full prehistoric and historical range covered in A.1.a.

Radar is the paradigmatic electromagnetic sensor addressed in outline F.1, covering its use in military communication, navigation, warning, detection, and weapon guidance through radio and microwave technology.

Encompasses the history of weapons technology evolution, doctrinal change, and military organization across all major categories of the outline — offensive weapons (A), defensive weapons (B), and weapon platforms (C) — making it a broad contextual entry for the section.

Covers surface warships from ancient vessels through steam-powered battleships and aircraft carriers (C.2.c) to diesel-powered craft (C.2.d) and nuclear-powered vessels (C.2.e), directly mapping to the outline's surface ship weapon platform subsections.

Directly covers the explosive devices category (A.3) — mines, depth charges, bombs, and warheads — distinguishing high explosives from low explosives and primary from secondary types, and addresses propellant chemistry relevant to A.2.e.i.

Military engineering (D) — tactical support through fortification, strategic support through roads, bridges, and ports, and ancillary support through maps and bomb disposal — is a direct application of engineering as covered in this article.

Metallurgy underpins weapon construction materials from bronze and iron for early weapons (A.1.a) to cast iron and forged steel for gunpowder weapons (A.2.a), and from mail and plate armor (B.1.a) to modern steel and composite ballistic protection (B.1.b).

The military article covers logistics — supply, transport, lodging, and services — directly corresponding to outline section E, as well as personnel hierarchy, training, and technology procurement that contextualizes all the technical sections.

Nuclear power covers fission and fusion energy sources (A.5.a) and nuclear-powered vessel propulsion for aircraft carriers and cruisers (C.2.e) and nuclear submarine propulsion (C.3.a), all directly relevant to the military technology outline.

Navigation covers radar, radio navigation, inertial navigation, and satellite-based systems — all within the electronics section on electromagnetic sensors and transmitters used in navigation (F.1) and supporting military engineering maps (D.3).

Lasers are explicitly included in outline F.1 as laser technology used in communication, navigation, warning/detection, and weapon guidance — covering directed-energy weapons and lidar applications in military electronics.

Maps are explicitly listed in outline D.3 as ancillary engineering support for military operations, covering cartographic elements essential to military navigation, terrain analysis, and operational planning.
